section.title: In the Lord I Take Refuge
section.sectionRationale: A short psalm of confidence answering the counsel to flee, in which David refuses to run from danger because the LORD is enthroned and watching, testing both righteous and wicked, and promising that the upright will see His face.
section.thesis: When counseled to flee like a bird because the foundations are being destroyed, David refuses, because the LORD is on His heavenly throne, testing every heart, and the upright, not the wicked, will ultimately see His face.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the counsel to flee and its rejection (vv1-3), the LORD's heavenly throne and His testing of all people (vv4-5), and the coming judgment on the wicked contrasted with the upright who see His face (vv6-7).

verses.text: In the LORD I {{chasah|take refuge}}. How then can you say to me: "Flee like a bird to your mountain!
For behold, the wicked bend their bows. They set their arrow on the string to shoot from the shadows at the upright in heart.
If the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?"

units.label: The LORD is on His throne

verses.text: The LORD is in His holy temple; the LORD is on His heavenly throne. His eyes are watching closely; they examine the sons of men.
The LORD tests the righteous and the wicked; His soul hates the lover of violence.

units.label: The upright will see His face
units.note: coals and sulfur for the wicked

verses.text: On the wicked He will rain down fiery coals and sulfur; a scorching wind will be their portion.
For the LORD is righteous; He loves justice. The upright will see His face.

chasah.gloss: To take refuge, to seek shelter, a favorite Psalter verb pictured as a bird or animal fleeing to a rock or covering for protection. David's declaration, in the LORD I take refuge, is the settled conviction that answers and refuses the counsel to flee elsewhere.

basic.context: Psalm 11 answers a specific, urgent counsel: with the wicked shooting arrows from the shadows and society's very foundations seeming to crumble, someone advises David to flee like a bird to the mountains. David refuses, because the LORD is enthroned in His holy temple, testing every heart, and the upright, not the wicked, will finally see His face.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a psalm of confidence responding to a real crisis, not a denial that danger exists; David does not pretend the arrows or the crumbling foundations are imaginary, but answers the fear of collapse with the settled fact of God's unshaken, watching throne.
basic.originalLanguages: David's opening declaration, ba'YHWH chasiti, in the LORD I take refuge, uses chasah, a verb pictured elsewhere as a bird sheltering under wings, directly answering the counsel to flee like a bird elsewhere. God's eyes 'examine,' bachan, the same verb used for testing metal in a furnace, a refining scrutiny applied to both righteous and wicked.
basic.typology: David's refusal to flee, resting instead in God's watching, testing throne, anticipates Christ, who did not flee His own hour of trial but trusted His Father's sovereign oversight completely, even to the cross.
basic.christ: Jesus refused the temptation to escape His appointed suffering, trusting the Father's sovereign throne exactly as David does here, and He is Himself the upright one who, though tested by fire, now sees the Father's face and secures that same sight for everyone united to Him.
basic.god: God is pictured here as both intimately near, in His holy temple, and cosmically sovereign, on His heavenly throne, watching every person closely with a testing gaze that examines hearts, hating violence yet loving justice and welcoming the upright to see His face.
basic.crossReferences: Habakkuk 2:20 echoes the LORD in His holy temple before whom all the earth should be silent. Hebrews 12:27-28 speaks of an unshakable kingdom amid the shaking of all else. Matthew 5:8 promises that the pure in heart will see God.
basic.oneStory: Psalm 11 answers every fear that the foundations are collapsing with the settled fact that God's throne never shakes; He watches, tests, and will finally welcome the upright to see His face, a hope secured completely in Jesus Christ, who did not flee but trusted the Father's throne through His own trial by fire.

context.literary: A brief but tightly structured psalm of confidence, opening with a quoted counsel to flee, answering it with a vision of God's unshaken heavenly throne, and closing with the contrasting destinies of the wicked and the upright.
context.historical: The superscription, for the choirmaster, a Psalm of David, gives no specific occasion, though its imagery of arrows shot from the shadows and crumbling foundations fits well with periods of instability during Saul's pursuit of David or times of social upheaval in Israel.
context.canonical: Part of a family of psalms of confidence, alongside Psalms 16, 23, and 27, that answer fear not by denying danger but by fixing attention on God's sovereign, watching presence.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer a specific counsel of despair, flee, because the foundations are destroyed, with a settled confidence in God's unshaken heavenly throne and His just, testing oversight of both righteous and wicked.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific moment of crisis and a specific counsel to flee; it prescribes for every believer facing similar collapse or threat the pattern of taking refuge in God's sovereignty rather than fleeing in panic, without claiming flight is always wrong in every circumstance.

debates.question: Who is speaking the counsel to flee in verses 1-3, David's own advisors or the wicked themselves?

views.label: Well-meaning advisors urging a pragmatic retreat
views.case: The counsel reads as sober, practical advice from people around David who see real danger, arrows from the shadows, and reasonably suggest flight as the wise course.

views.label: A taunt from the wicked designed to provoke fear and abandonment of trust
views.case: Given the psalm's contrast between the wicked's violence and David's refuge in God, some read the counsel to flee as coming from, or echoing, the very enemies described, intended to shake David's confidence.

debates.bearing: Either way, David's response is the same and instructive: he answers the counsel to flee, whatever its source, not by denying real danger but by anchoring his confidence in God's unshaken throne rather than in escape.

study.originalLanguages: David's opening confession, ba'YHWH chasiti, in the LORD I take refuge, employs chasah, a favorite Psalter verb pictured as a bird sheltering under a parent's wings, directly countering the counsel to flee like a bird elsewhere. God's examining eyes use bachan, the same verb applied to refining precious metal by fire, describing a scrutiny that tests the true nature of both righteous and wicked alike.

typology.type: The refusal to flee, resting instead in the Father's sovereign, watching throne, Psalm 11:1, 4
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who did not escape His appointed hour of trial but entrusted Himself completely to the Father's sovereign will, even to the cross, Luke 22:42.

study.christ: David refuses the counsel to flee, resting instead in the LORD's unshaken heavenly throne; Jesus lived this trust perfectly, refusing every opportunity to escape His appointed suffering, Matthew 26:53, and entrusting Himself entirely to His Father's sovereign will even to the cross. Christ is also the truly upright one, tested completely and vindicated, who now sees the Father's face and, by uniting believers to Himself, secures for them the very promise this psalm makes: the upright will see His face.
study.god: God is near and cosmic at once, in His holy temple and on His heavenly throne, watching every person with an examining, testing gaze; He hates the lover of violence yet loves justice, and His righteous character is precisely what makes Him trustworthy enough to take refuge in when the foundations themselves seem to shake.

crossReferences.note: Echoes the LORD in His holy temple before whom all the earth should keep silence, the same image of God's settled, sovereign presence.
Describes an unshakable kingdom that remains when everything else is shaken, echoing this psalm's confidence amid crumbling foundations.
Promises that the pure in heart will see God, echoing this psalm's closing promise that the upright will see His face.

study.oneStory: Psalm 11 answers every counsel of despair with the settled fact of God's unshaken throne, watching, testing, and finally welcoming the upright into His presence. Jesus Christ refused to flee His own appointed trial, trusting that same sovereign throne, and now secures for every believer the promise that they too will see God's face.

sermon.title: Runaway World
sermon.why: Begg preaches through Psalm 11:1-7, urging believers to learn from the tumultuous life of King David that we will only endure life's difficulties and Satan's snares if God is our refuge, rather than running from our troubles.

quiz.q: What counsel does David receive at the start of Psalm 11?

options.text: To flee like a bird to the mountains, since the wicked are shooting arrows and the foundations are being destroyed.
options.note: This is the specific counsel of despair the psalm opens by quoting.

options.text: To attack his enemies directly.
options.note: The counsel given is to flee, not to attack; David's response is neither fleeing nor attacking but taking refuge in God.

options.text: To offer a sacrifice for forgiveness.
options.note: No sacrifice is mentioned; the counsel specifically urges flight from danger.

options.text: To seek an alliance with a foreign king.
options.note: This is not the counsel described; the specific advice given is to flee to the mountains.

quiz.q: How does David respond to this counsel?

options.text: He declares that he takes refuge in the LORD rather than fleeing.
options.note: This is David's opening declaration and settled response to the counsel to flee.

options.text: He immediately flees as advised.
options.note: David explicitly rejects this counsel, declaring his refuge is in the LORD instead.

options.text: He ignores the danger entirely.
options.note: David does not deny the danger is real; he answers it with trust in God rather than denial or flight.

options.text: He gives no response at all.
options.note: Verse 1 is a direct, explicit response rejecting the counsel to flee.

quiz.q: What does the LORD do, according to verses 4-5?

options.text: He sits enthroned in His holy temple and heavenly throne, His eyes watching closely and testing both the righteous and the wicked.
options.note: This describes God's sovereign, attentive oversight of all humanity.

options.text: He is absent from His temple during times of crisis.
options.note: The text explicitly affirms God's presence and active watching, not absence.

options.text: He tests only the wicked, never the righteous.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly says the LORD tests both the righteous and the wicked.

options.text: He has no interest in human affairs.
options.note: The text describes God's eyes closely watching and examining, the opposite of disinterest.

quiz.q: What is promised to the wicked in verse 6?

options.text: The LORD will rain down fiery coals and sulfur on them, and a scorching wind will be their portion.
options.note: This vivid image of judgment describes the wicked's coming fate.

options.text: They will prosper permanently with no consequence.
options.note: The text explicitly describes coming judgment, not permanent prosperity.

options.text: They will be given a final chance to repent with no judgment described.
options.note: Verse 6 describes specific judgment, not merely an offer of repentance.

options.text: Nothing; the psalm makes no mention of judgment for the wicked.
options.note: Verse 6 explicitly describes coming judgment on the wicked.

quiz.q: What is promised to the upright in verse 7?

options.text: They will see His face, because the LORD is righteous and loves justice.
options.note: This is the psalm's closing promise for the upright, contrasted with the wicked's judgment.

options.text: They will receive great material wealth.
options.note: The promise given is specifically to see God's face, not a promise of material wealth.

options.text: They will never face any hardship again.
options.note: The promise is relational and future, seeing God's face, not a guarantee against present hardship.

options.text: Nothing is promised to the upright.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly promises the upright will see His face.

questions: David is counseled to flee from danger, but instead declares his refuge is in the LORD. When you face pressure to run from a hard situation, what would it look like for you to take refuge in God instead of simply escaping?

David does not deny that real arrows are being shot and real foundations are shaking; he answers real danger with real trust. Where in your life do you need to hold both truths together, honest acknowledgment of danger and confident trust in God?
The LORD's eyes examine and test both the righteous and the wicked, like a refiner testing metal. What is God currently testing or refining in your own heart through your present circumstances?

The psalm contrasts the coming judgment of the wicked with the promise that the upright will see God's face. How does the hope of one day seeing God face to face shape the way you endure present difficulty?

Jesus refused every opportunity to escape His appointed suffering, trusting His Father's sovereign throne completely. How does His example strengthen you to stay faithful in a situation you are currently tempted to flee?

translationNotes.note: Flee like a bird to your mountain pictures a bird escaping to a rocky crag for safety, a common image for retreat to a place of natural refuge, here contrasted with David's chosen refuge in the LORD Himself.

His eyes are watching closely, they examine renders a Hebrew verb, bachan, elsewhere used for testing or refining metal by fire, describing God's scrutiny as a discerning, purifying examination rather than mere observation.

The upright will see His face is among the earliest scriptural expressions of the hope, developed more fully later in Scripture, of direct, unmediated fellowship with God as the believer's final reward, echoed in Matthew 5:8 and Revelation 22:4.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 11:1-7. No em or en dashes appear in this psalm's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file. Sermon link verified this session by fetching the Truth For Life sermon page for Psalm 11.
